mnemonics: Fast Cars Pass Quickly (FCPQ) for Fibonacci, Coin Change, and Partition (Quickselect) problems
keyPoints: Fibonacci exemplifies simple recursion with exponential time and how memoization/dynamic programming optimizes it to linear time.
The Coin Change problem is a classic example of a dynamic programming solution for finding the minimum number of coins to make a given amount.
The Partition algorithm (used in Quickselect) demonstrates a linear-time O(n) routine for dividing an array around a pivot, which is fundamental for efficient sorting and selection.
commonMistakes: Confusing the greedy algorithm approach with the dynamic programming solution for the Coin Change problem (greedy doesn't work for arbitrary coin systems).
Implementing the recursive Fibonacci without memoization, leading to an inefficient O(2^n) algorithm instead of the optimal O(n) DP solution.
Making an off-by-one error in the Partition algorithm's indices or incorrectly handling the pivot element's final position.
